Name: curvature-aware_precision_routing
Title: Training-Free Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: A Geometric Subspace Approach
Short Hypothesis: Curvature-aware precision routing can be achieved training-free by leveraging geometric subspace diagnostics and spectral quantization theory, enabling deterministic optimization proxies with formalized stability radii.
Related Work: Existing work, such as Tri-Accel, focuses on training-time adaptive precision routing using curvature and gradient variance. This proposal differs by eliminating the need for training, instead relying on geometric subspace diagnostics and spectral quantization to formalize stability radii. This approach aligns with foundational optimization theory and prioritizes mathematical transparency over heuristic adaptations.
Abstract: We propose a training-free precision routing framework that leverages curvature-aware geometric subspace diagnostics and spectral quantization theory. By quantifying gradient covariance decay, Hessian conditioning, and feature entanglement, our approach replaces heuristic precision switching with formalized stability radii and explicit condition number constraints. The framework enforces deterministic execution pathways through coarse-grained, block-level precision tiering, ensuring strict overhead bounds and architectural coherence. Empirical validation on single-GPU setups demonstrates significant improvements in numerical stability and computational efficiency, with explicit PAC-Bayes generalization bounds and curvature transparency. This work bridges theoretical ML with scalable inference, offering a resource-efficient and mathematically grounded alternative to training-based precision routing.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Validate curvature-aware precision routing on ResNet-18 and EfficientNet-B0 using CIFAR-10/100. Measure improvements in numerical stability (condition number) and computational efficiency (training-free overhead).
Experiment 2: Compare formalized stability radii against heuristic precision switching strategies. Evaluate using PAC-Bayes generalization bounds and curvature transparency metrics.
Experiment 3: Test coarse-grained, block-level precision tiering on single-GPU setups. Measure overhead bounds and deterministic execution pathways.

Experiment 4: Quantify gradient covariance decay, Hessian conditioning, and feature entanglement across multiple architectures (e.g., Transformer, ResNet). Use spectral estimators and pre-execution diagnostic loops.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Limited applicability to architectures with highly non-linear curvature profiles.
Potential overhead from pre-execution diagnostic loops in resource-constrained environments.
Reliance on deterministic execution pathways may limit scalability in distributed setups.
Empirical validation restricted to single-GPU environments, which may not generalize to multi-GPU or edge devices.
